title: Finetuned Language Models are Zero-Shot Learners

structured.summary: This paper proposes FLAN, a 137B-parameter language model fine-tuned on over 60 NLP datasets using natural language instructions to improve zero-shot generalization on unseen tasks. Instruction tuning enables FLAN to outperform zero- and few-shot GPT-3 on many benchmarks, demonstrating that instruction-based finetuning substantially enhances zero-shot learning for large language models.

strengths.point: Instruction tuning substantially improves zero-shot learning on diverse unseen tasks
strengths.evidence: FLAN outperforms zero-shot GPT-3 on 20 out of 25 datasets and even few-shot GPT-3 on several tasks such as ANLI, RTE, and QA.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical analysis with ablations on model scale, task diversity, and instruction usage
strengths.evidence: Ablations show larger model scale (~100B+), multi-task diversity, and natural language instructions are critical for gains.

strengths.point: Novel evaluation setup using leave-one-cluster-out to test true zero-shot generalization
strengths.evidence: Datasets are grouped into 12 task clusters, with zero-shot evaluation on held-out clusters to avoid task leakage.

strengths.point: Release of instruction tuning datasets and code to facilitate reproducibility and future work
strengths.evidence: The paper provides resources and templates for instruction tuning across many datasets.

weaknesses.point: Lack of direct comparison or detailed discussion with concurrent instruction-tuning methods
weaknesses.evidence: Minimal analysis contrasting FLAN with related works like T0 or other multi-task instruction-tuned models limits clarity on novelty.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient analysis of sensitivity to instruction wording variations
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments vary instruction phrasing or length to assess robustness and reproducibility of instruction tuning.

weaknesses.point: Unclear justification and stability of dataset cluster partitioning
weaknesses.evidence: The criteria for forming 12 task clusters and their impact on zero-shot evaluation are not well explained.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation comparisons with GPT-3 lack full prompt and decoding details
weaknesses.evidence: Prompt templates, option suffixes, and decoding hyperparameters used for GPT-3 are not fully disclosed, possibly confounding performance claims.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility limited by sparse details on instruction templates and training hyperparameters
weaknesses.evidence: Few instruction examples and minimal tuning details for large models make exact replication difficult.

weaknesses.point: Narrow scope of instruction complexity and limited language/task generalizability
weaknesses.evidence: Instructions are mostly short and simple; evaluation focuses on English and standard NLP tasks, with little on low-resource languages or complex instructions.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on ethical risks and potential bias amplification from instruction tuning
weaknesses.evidence: Ethical considerations are briefly mentioned but lack analysis of harms or mitigation strategies.

weaknesses.point: Negative impact on smaller models and few-shot gains confounded by model size not fully explored
weaknesses.evidence: Degradation on <8B models after instruction tuning and comparisons with larger GPT-3 lack deeper investigation.

questions.question: How robust is FLAN to variations in the wording, length, or complexity of instructions?
questions.motivation: Understanding sensitivity to instruction formulation is critical for reproducibility and adapting instruction tuning to new tasks.

questions.question: How does FLAN perform on truly out-of-distribution tasks, including low-resource languages and multi-modal inputs?
questions.motivation: Demonstrating generalization beyond English and standard benchmarks would validate broader applicability.

questions.question: Can instruction tuning be effectively scaled or adapted for smaller models without performance degradation?
questions.motivation: Smaller models are more practical to deploy; mitigating their negative tuning effects is important.

questions.question: What are the ethical implications of instruction tuning on biased datasets, and how can potential harms be mitigated?
questions.motivation: Widespread deployment demands understanding and reducing bias propagation and dual-use risks.

questions.question: How does FLAN compare to other instruction tuning approaches like T0 in terms of dataset selection, training procedure, and zero-shot performance?
questions.motivation: Clarifying novelty and advantages over concurrent work aids the community in selecting methods.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of direct comparison or detailed discussion with concurrent instruction-tuning methods. Minimal analysis contrasting FLAN with related works like T0 or other multi-task instruction-tuned models limits clarity on novelty
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient analysis of sensitivity to instruction wording variations. No experiments vary instruction phrasing or length to assess robustness and reproducibility of instruction tuning
critique_points.point_003: Unclear justification and stability of dataset cluster partitioning. The criteria for forming 12 task clusters and their impact on zero-shot evaluation are not well explained
critique_points.point_004: Evaluation comparisons with GPT-3 lack full prompt and decoding details. Prompt templates, option suffixes, and decoding hyperparameters used for GPT-3 are not fully disclosed, possibly confounding performance claims
critique_points.point_005: Reproducibility limited by sparse details on instruction templates and training hyperparameters. Few instruction examples and minimal tuning details for large models make exact replication difficult
critique_points.point_006: Narrow scope of instruction complexity and limited language/task generalizability. Instructions are mostly short and simple; evaluation focuses on English and standard NLP tasks, with little on low-resource languages or complex instructions
critique_points.point_007: Limited discussion on ethical risks and potential bias amplification from instruction tuning. Ethical considerations are briefly mentioned but lack analysis of harms or mitigation strategies
critique_points.point_008: Negative impact on smaller models and few-shot gains confounded by model size not fully explored. Degradation on <8B models after instruction tuning and comparisons with larger GPT-3 lack deeper investigation
